perturbation.original_text: 4.1 For purposes of this Agreement, the term " Affiliate " shall mean any person which either directly or indirectly controls, is directly or indirectly controlled by, 3 or is under common control with a Party and, for purposes of t his definition, a person shall be deemed to "control" an entity if it owns or has the ability to vote, directly or indirectly, more than 50% of the voting securities or equivalent interest of that entity and a reference to an Affiliate shall include a refe rence to such Affiliate's employees, officers, directors , representatives and advis ers.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 For purposes of this Agreement, the term "Affiliate" shall mean any person or entity that directly controls the Party. However, for clauses related to financial liability only, an "Affiliate" shall be defined as any person which either directly or indirectly controls, is directly or indirectly controlled by, 3 or is under common control with a Party and, for purposes of t his definition, a person shall be deemed to "control" an entity if it owns or has the ability to vote, directly or indirectly, more than 50% of the voting securities or equivalent interest of that entity and a reference to an Affiliate shall include a refe rence to such Affiliate's employees, officers, directors , representatives and advis ers.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a contradiction in the definition of 'Affiliate.' While the initial part defines it as any entity that directly controls the Party for most purposes, it expands the definition for clauses related to financial liability to include entities under indirect or common control. This contradicts the restrictive definition in other clauses, creating ambiguity about which entities are considered affiliates for non-financial obligations. This creates a direct in-text contradiction with respect to the scope of 'Affiliate' and its application.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that the unauthorised use or disclosure of any Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party may give rise to substantial damage to the Disclosing Party . Accordingly , the Receiving Party hereby undertakes to the Disclosing Party that, for as long as this Agreement remains in force, it will (and it will procure that its employees, officers, directors, representatives , advise rs and any Affiliate to which it discloses the Confidential Information will) – 4.2.1 treat as confidential all Confidential Information; 4.2.2 protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information in the same manner and with the same degree of care that a reasonable man would use to protect his own confidential information; 4.2.3 not use, exploit or in any other manner whatsoever apply or reproduce the Confidential Information except to the extent required to achieve the Purpose; 4.2.4 not divulge or permit access to the Confiden tial Information to any person except to those of its employees , officers, directors , representatives , advisers and Affiliates who have a specific need to know such information in relation to the Transaction . Before revealing any Confidential Information to its employees , officers, directors, representatives , advisers and/or Affiliates , the Receiving Party shall be obliged to notify such employees , officers, directors, representatives , advisers and/or Affiliates of the confidential nature of t he disclosure and ensure that such employees , officers, directors, representatives , advisers and/or Affiliates are obliged not to disclose the Confidential Information to any unauthorised third party and not use the Confidential Information except as may b e strictly required in relation to the Transaction . The Receiving Party shall be liable for any damages incurred by the Disclosing Party as a result of any unauthorised disclosure of the Confidential Information made by the Receiving Party, it s employees, officers, directors, representatives and /or Affiliates; and 4.2.5 immediately notify the Disclosing Party in writing if it becomes aware that there has been an unauthorised disclosure or use of any Confidential Information and take all necessary steps to recov er such Confidential Information and to prevent any further disclosure of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by defining 'Affiliate' narrowly for general purposes ('any entity that directly controls the Party') but expanding it for financial liability clauses to include entities under indirect or common control. This directly conflicts with the contradicted text (4.2.4) which states that the Receiving Party must ensure that its 'Affiliates' are obliged not to disclose Confidential Information, implying a consistent definition of 'Affiliate' throughout the agreement. The change in definition creates ambiguity and contradiction about which entities are considered affiliates for the purpose of confidentiality obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The Disclosing Party may, at any time, request the Receiving Party to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party , its Affiliates or any of their respective employees, representatives, of ficers, directors or advis ers, in relation to the Transaction and may, in addition, request the Receiving Party to furnish a written statement sworn under oath to the effect that, upon such return, neither the Receiving Party nor any of its Affiliates have retained in their possession, or under their control, either directly or indirectly, any such material or any copies thereof or extracts therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The Disclosing Party may, at any time, request the Receiving Party to return any tangible material containing, pertaining to or relating to Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party , its Affiliates or any of their respective employees, representatives, of ficers, directors or advis ers, in relation to the Transaction and may, in addition, request the Receiving Party to furnish a written statement sworn under oath to the effect that, upon such return, neither the Receiving Party nor any of its Affiliates have retained in their possession, or under their control, either directly or indirectly, any such tangible material or any copies thereof or extracts therefrom. However, electronic copies of Confidential Information need not be returned or destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation adds the word 'tangible' to limit the return requirement only to physical materials. Simultaneously, it specifies that electronic copies don't need to be returned or destroyed. This introduces an in-text contradiction because the original clause encompassed all forms of Confidential Information, while the modified clause creates an exception for electronic copies. This can be exploited if the Receiving Party claims that the confidentiality undertaking does not apply to information stored electronically.
perturbation.contradicted_text: treat as confidential all Confidential Information;
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 6.1 required the return of *any* material containing Confidential Information. The changed text modifies this by limiting the return requirement to 'tangible' material and explicitly excluding electronic copies. This directly contradicts the general confidentiality obligation ('treat as confidential all Confidential Information') because the receiving party is no longer obligated to return or destroy electronic copies of confidential information, creating a loophole where electronic information is not treated as confidential, which is a direct violation of the contradicted text. This is a strong contradiction that impacts enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 7 NON-SOLICITATION OF EMPL OYEES The Receiving Party agrees that , save as contemplated in any definitive transaction agreement concluded between the Parties for purposes of the Transaction, it will not, for a period of 24 months a fter the Signature Date, directly or indirectly, and on its own behalf or on behalf of any other person, encourage, entice, induce or persuade any person - 7.1 who is, as at the Signature Date or during the course of the Parties discussions relating to the Pu rpose, in the employ of the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates ; and 7.2 with whom the Receiving Party has had or may have had any contact in relation to the Confidential Information or whose name and/or any other details are included in the Confidential I nformation, to terminate such employment, nor shall it encourage or support any of its Affiliates to do so. This 7 shall not preclude the Receivin g Party or its Affiliates from employing any such employee (a) who seeks employment by contacting the Receiving Party on his or her own initiative or in response to any general advertisement or (b) who is identified as a prospective employee by an independ ent third party such as an employment agency in the normal course of such agency's business and not at the Receiving Party's direction.
perturbation.changed_text: 7 NON-SOLICITATION OF EMPL OYEES The Receiving Party agrees that , save as contemplated in any definitive transaction agreement concluded between the Parties for purposes of the Transaction, it will not, for a period of 6 months a fter the Signature Date, directly or indirectly, and on its own behalf or on behalf of any other person, encourage, entice, induce or persuade any person - 7.1 who is, as at the Signature Date or during the course of the Parties discussions relating to the Pu rpose, in the employ of the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates ; and 7.2 with whom the Receiving Party has had or may have had any contact in relation to the Confidential Information or whose name and/or any other details are included in the Confidential I nformation, to terminate such employment, nor shall it encourage or support any of its Affiliates to do so. This 7 shall not preclude the Receivin g Party or its Affiliates from employing any such employee (a) who seeks employment by contacting the Receiving Party on his or her own initiative or in response to any general advertisement or (b) who is identified as a prospective employee by an independ ent third party such as an employment agency in the normal course of such agency's business and not at the Receiving Party's direction. However, after those 6 months, the Receiving Party is free to solicit the Disclosing Party’s employee, with a written notice of that intention to the Disclosing Party
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation initially reduces the non-solicitation period from 24 months to 6 months. Then, it adds that after those 6 months, the Receiving Party can solicit employees with written notice. This creates an internal contradiction: While the initial clause intends to protect the Disclosing Party's employees for a set period, the subsequent clause undermines this intention by allowing solicitation with notice after a short period. The in-text contradiction lies in the reduced effectiveness of non-solicitation due to the easy allowance of solicitation with notice.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7 NON-SOLICITATION OF EMPL OYEES The Receiving Party agrees that , save as contemplated in any definitive transaction agreement concluded between the Parties for purposes of the Transaction, it will not, for a period of 6 months a fter the Signature Date, directly or indirectly, and on its own behalf or on behalf of any other person, encourage, entice, induce or persuade any person - 7.1 who is, as at the Signature Date or during the course of the Parties discussions relating to the Pu rpose, in the employ of the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates ; and 7.2 with whom the Receiving Party has had or may have had any contact in relation to the Confidential Information or whose name and/or any other details are included in the Confidential I nformation, to terminate such employment, nor shall it encourage or support any of its Affiliates to do so. This 7 shall not preclude the Receivin g Party or its Affiliates from employing any such employee (a) who seeks employment by contacting the Receiving Party on his or her own initiative or in response to any general advertisement or (b) who is identified as a prospective employee by an independ ent third party such as an employment agency in the normal course of such agency's business and not at the Receiving Party's direction. However, after those 6 months, the Receiving Party is free to solicit the Disclosing Party’s employee, with a written notice of that intention to the Disclosing Party
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the non-solicitation period from 24 months to 6 months and adds a clause allowing solicitation after 6 months with written notice. This directly contradicts the original intent of the non-solicitation clause, which was to protect the Disclosing Party's employees for a longer period. The contradicted text reflects this change, making it free to solicit after the 6 month period, thus leading to an in-text contradiction, since the initial intention of a strict non-solicitation period is undermined by the added clause.

perturbation.original_text: 13.4 In the event of any dispute or difference arising between the Parties r elating to or arising out of this Agreement, including the implementation, execution, interpretation, rectification or cancellation of this Agreement, the dispute shall be referred for arbitration to the Arbitration Foundation of Southern Africa (or its su ccessor -in-title) ("AFSA ") in terms of AFSA 's Rules of Arbitration for the time being in force. The seat, or legal place, of the arbitration shall be Johannesburg , Republic of South Africa and the language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be E nglish.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.4 In the event of any dispute or difference arising between the Parties relating to or arising out of this Agreement, including the implementation, execution, interpretation, rectification or cancellation of this Agreement, disputes involving Confidential Information shall be resolved through confidential binding arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Act of 1965 (South Africa). All other disputes shall be resolved by AFSA. The seat, or legal place, of the arbitration shall be Johannesburg, Republic of South Africa and the language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a contradiction by creating two different dispute resolution mechanisms. Disputes involving Confidential Information are mandated to go through arbitration per the Arbitration Act of 1965, while all other disputes are handled by AFSA. The contradiction arises because it's not always clear whether a dispute involves Confidential Information, potentially leading to legal challenges over the correct forum for dispute resolution. The in-text contradiction creates uncertainty and potential legal challenges regarding dispute resolution procedures.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event of any dispute or difference arising between the Parties relating to or arising out of this Agreement, including the implementation, execution, interpretation, rectification or cancellation of this Agreement, disputes involving Confidential Information shall be resolved through confidential binding arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Act of 1965 (South Africa). All other disputes shall be resolved by AFSA. The seat, or legal place, of the arbitration shall be Johannesburg, Republic of South Africa and the language to be used in the arbitral proceedings shall be English.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces two different dispute resolution mechanisms: one for disputes involving confidential information (Arbitration Act of 1965) and another for all other disputes (AFSA). The contradicted text is the same as the changed text. This creates a direct conflict because it becomes unclear which mechanism applies when a dispute involves confidential information, potentially invalidating either or both mechanisms. This adheres to the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.
